perturbation.original_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for Bucks County, Pennsylvania to enforce the terms of this Agreement. Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter. All other claims must be arbitrated in accordance with section 11 below.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The performance and construction of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania. All sums due hereunder shall be payable at the office of the Broker in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for Bucks County, Pennsylvania to enforce the terms of this Agreement. All other claims must be arbitrated in accordance with section 11 below.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'Claims for injunctive relief may be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction over the matter' limits the buyer's ability to seek immediate legal recourse through injunctions, potentially violating Pennsylvania's consumer protection laws ensuring access to equitable remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification removes the buyer's explicit right to seek injunctive relief in any court with appropriate jurisdiction, which can be interpreted as hindering their ability to pursue remedies guaranteed under Pennsylvania's consumer protection laws. 73 P.S. § 201-9.2 allows private actions, which implicitly include the right to seek equitable relief such as injunctions. By limiting the ability to pursue injunctive relief, the NDA potentially violates the spirit and intent of this law.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed claims for injunctive relief to be filed in any court with appropriate jurisdiction, whereas the changed text removes this option. This removal hinders the buyer's ability to pursue remedies, which consumer protection laws are intended to provide. The Perturbation Explanation states that this could violate Pennsylvania's consumer protection laws ensuring access to equitable remedies. The Law Explanation states that this violates 73 P.S. § 201-9.2 which allows private actions including the right to seek equitable relief such as injunctions. Therefore, the removal of this clause contradicts the intent of consumer protection laws, constituting a legally significant contradiction based on the omission.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement. The expenses of arbitration conducted pursuant to this paragraph shall be born by the parties in such proportion as the Arbitrator shall decide.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement. The expenses of arbitration conducted pursuant to this paragraph shall be born by the parties in such proportion as the Arbitrator shall decide.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'on written request of one (1) party served on the other', the contract now mandates mandatory arbitration, potentially violating Pennsylvania law which disfavors mandatory arbitration clauses, particularly in consumer contracts, where it can be argued there's unequal bargaining power. This makes the arbitration clause unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification imposes mandatory arbitration by removing the need for written request, which could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent the common law principles and statutory framework that require voluntary and informed consent to arbitration, especially in situations where there is a significant power imbalance between the parties. While Pennsylvania law generally upholds arbitration agreements, courts scrutinize agreements where one party is compelled to arbitrate without a clear and knowing waiver of their right to a jury trial. The removal of 'on written request of one (1) party served on the other' can be considered a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304 (2022) - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 73 - Arbitration Section 7304 - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 7304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 7304. Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration. (a) Compelling arbitration.-- On application to a court to compel arbitration made by a party showing an agreement described in section 7303 (relating to validity of agreement to arbitrate) and a showing that an opposing party refused to arbitrate, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. If the opposing party denies the existence of an agreement to arbitrate, the court shall proceed summarily to determine the issue so raised and shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration if it finds for the moving party. Otherwise, the application shall be denied. (b) Stay of arbitration.-- On application of a party to a court to stay an arbitration proceeding threatened or commenced the court may stay an arbitration on a showing that there is no agreement to arbitrate. When in substantial and bona fide dispute, such an issue shall be forthwith and summarily tried and determined and a stay of the arbitration proceedings shall be ordered if the court finds for the moving party. If the court finds for the opposing party, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. (c) Venue.-- If a controversy alleged to be or not to be referable to arbitration under the agreeme
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'written request of one (1) party served on the other' to initiate arbitration. The changed contract removes this requirement, making arbitration mandatory. This contradicts the principle that arbitration should be based on a voluntary agreement. The law snippet, 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304, outlines court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration. Section 7304(b) allows a party to apply to stay arbitration if 'there is no agreement to arbitrate'. By removing the written request clause, the changed contract weakens the ability of a party to argue there was no agreement to arbitrate, thereby contradicting the protections afforded by this law. The absence of a specific consumer protection law in the snippets doesn't negate the fact that making arbitration mandatory potentially undermines the voluntary nature of the agreement, which is implicitly protected by the statute outlining the process for compelling or staying arbitration.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including, without limitation, reasonable attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'without limitation, reasonable' from the indemnification clause makes it overly broad. Under Pennsylvania law, indemnification clauses must be reasonable and not cover actions stemming from the indemnitee's own negligence or misconduct. This omission creates a potential for the buyer to be responsible for unreasonable expenses, which violates Pennsylvania's principles of fair dealing and contract interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires indemnification agreements to be 'strictly construed' and 'not interpreted to provide indemnity for the indemnitee's own negligence unless such intent is expressed in clear and unequivocal language'. Removing the word 'reasonable' and 'without limitation' expands the scope of the indemnification beyond what is permissible under Pennsylvania law, particularly if it can be construed as requiring the buyer to indemnify the Broker and Seller for their own negligence or misconduct. The omission weakens the protection afforded to the buyer and introduces a potential violation of Pennsylvania contract law.

perturbation.justification: The omission of "without limitation, reasonable" from the indemnification clause potentially broadens the buyer's responsibility to cover unreasonable expenses, which contradicts principles of fair dealing and contract interpretation. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that removing 'reasonable' and 'without limitation' expands the scope of indemnification beyond what is permissible, especially if it could require the buyer to indemnify the Broker and Seller for their negligence. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is just a sign in page, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest a violation of Pennsylvania contract law due to the omission, creating a clear potential for the buyer to be responsible for unreasonable expenses. This suggests the modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Information furnished by Broker has been prepared by or is based upon representations of the Seller and Broker has made no independent investigation or verification of said Information. Broker makes no representation that the Information is accurate or complete. Buyer hereby expressly releases and discharges Broker from any and all responsibility and/or liability in connection with the accuracy, completeness or any other aspects of the information and accepts sole and final responsibility for the evaluation of the Information and all other factors relating to the Business. Buyer understands and acknowledges that it has been advised to engage attorneys, accountants and other advisors to complete due diligence on the Business offered for sale to verify that the Business is satisfactory. Buyer understands that the future success or failure of the Business is primarily dependent on the Buyer’s knowledge and efforts and is not dependent on the Business’s past volume of business or profitability.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Information furnished by Broker has been prepared by or is based upon representations of the Seller and Broker has made no independent investigation or verification of said Information. Broker makes no representation that the Information is accurate or complete. Buyer hereby expressly releases and discharges Broker from any and all responsibility and/or liability in connection with the accuracy, completeness or any other aspects of the information and accepts sole and final responsibility for the evaluation of the Information and all other factors relating to the Business. Buyer understands that the future success or failure of the Business is primarily dependent on the Buyer’s knowledge and efforts and is not dependent on the Business’s past volume of business or profitability.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence, 'Buyer understands and acknowledges that it has been advised to engage attorneys, accountants and other advisors to complete due diligence on the Business offered for sale to verify that the Business is satisfactory' creates an imbalance of power. Pennsylvania consumer law emphasizes fair dealing and full disclosure. By removing the explicit advice to seek independent verification, the contract risks misleading the buyer into thinking due diligence is unnecessary, which goes against the principle of informed consent and consumer protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law
perturbation.law_explanation: 73 P.S. § 201-2(4) defines unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. While the original clause provides a safeguard by advising the buyer to perform due diligence, removing this creates the potential for the buyer to rely solely on the Broker's and Seller's representations. This omission could be interpreted as a deceptive practice if the information provided is inaccurate or incomplete, and the buyer, without independent verification, suffers damages. Removing the advice to seek independent verification can thus be seen as a violation of the UTPCPL, particularly sections regarding misrepresentation and failure to disclose material facts.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the sentence advising the buyer to seek independent verification contradicts consumer protection principles. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that removing this advice creates a risk of misleading the buyer into thinking due diligence is unnecessary, conflicting with informed consent. The Law Explanation supports this, suggesting the omission can be interpreted as a deceptive practice if the information provided is inaccurate and the buyer suffers damages without independent verification. Since no law snippets were provided, the decision is based on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, which outline a potential violation of consumer protection laws due to the omission of a crucial piece of advice that promotes informed decision-making.
